Name: rate_distortion_adaptive_compression
Title: Adaptive Rate-Distortion Compression Based on Mutual Information Dynamics
Short Hypothesis: The dynamic allocation of bits in model compression, guided by real-time mutual information estimations, can significantly improve the predictive sufficiency of compressed models while respecting rate-distortion constraints.
Related Work: Existing works such as 'Rate Distortion via Constrained Estimated Mutual Information Minimization' and 'Probabilistic Automated Model Compression via Representation Mutual Information Optimization' have advanced the understanding of rate-distortion theory in compression. However, they typically employ static models or assumptions regarding mutual information, failing to dynamically adapt during inference based on real-time data distributions. Our approach distinguishes itself by continuously estimating and adapting to the mutual information between model parameters and task performance during the compression process.
Abstract: In this proposal, we introduce a novel framework for adaptive model compression that leverages real-time mutual information dynamics to optimize bit allocation under rate-distortion constraints. Unlike traditional static approaches, our method employs a continuous estimation of mutual information between model weights and task performance, allowing for a more flexible and efficient allocation of resources. We hypothesize that by dynamically adjusting the compression strategy based on the changing data distribution, it is possible to achieve better predictive sufficiency while maintaining lower distortion. We will validate this hypothesis through a series of experiments that measure the performance of our adaptive compression method against conventional static methods, demonstrating superior efficiency in preserving task-relevant information. Our findings will contribute to a deeper understanding of the relationship between information theory and model compression, providing new insights into the operational limits of compression techniques.
Experiments: 1. Implement a baseline model using static bit allocation based on conventional rate-distortion principles.
2. Develop an adaptive model that employs real-time mutual information estimations to adjust bit allocation dynamically during inference.
3. Evaluate both models on various datasets with controlled distortion measures and assess performance using metrics such as accuracy, F1 score, and mutual information retention.
4. Conduct ablation studies to isolate the contributions of dynamic mutual information estimation versus static allocation.
5. Analyze the distortion-rate curves for both approaches to highlight the efficiency gains of the adaptive model.
Risk Factors and Limitations: 1. Potential computational overhead from continuous mutual information estimation may negate some of the efficiency gains.
2. The effectiveness of the method may be limited by the accuracy of the mutual information estimators employed.
3. The dynamic model may be sensitive to noise in the data, potentially leading to overfitting in certain scenarios.
